image.alt: Illustrated map of central Berlin Mitte showing the BerlinWalk tour route from Alexanderplatz to Hackescher Markt

stops.name: World Clock
stops.hook: Where the tour begins. A Cold War-era monument under which generations of Berliners have met without giving an address.

stops.name: TV Tower
stops.hook: East Berlin's tallest statement. Built in 1969 to outshine the West, with a reflective cross the Pope's Revenge story explains.

stops.name: Rotes Rathaus
stops.hook: Red brick that has watched Berlin become Berlin since 1869. Empire, war, division, reunification — all from one balcony.

stops.name: Neptun Brunnen
stops.hook: Four river goddesses around a sea god in the middle of Berlin. The detail no one notices until you stop to look.

stops.name: St. Mary's Church
stops.hook: Berlin's second oldest church, with a medieval dance-of-death fresco inside that survived everything the 20th century threw at it.

stops.name: Marx-Engels Forum
stops.hook: Two seated philosophers, a bit weathered. The plaza around them tells a longer story than the statue itself.

stops.name: Humboldt Forum
stops.hook: Demolished, then rebuilt as a near-replica 60 years later. The most controversial reconstruction in modern Berlin.

stops.name: Lustgarten
stops.hook: Royal park, military parade ground, Nazi rally site, and finally a garden again. Same lawn, very different uses.

stops.name: Berliner Dom
stops.hook: Prussian ambition in stone. The Hohenzollern family vault below, the green dome above, the bullet holes still on the facade.

stops.name: Altes Museum
stops.hook: Berlin's first public museum, 1830. Eighteen Ionic columns built to convince a young city it deserved to keep ancient treasures.

stops.name: Neues Museum & Alte Nationalgalerie
stops.hook: Nefertiti's home and a temple of 19th-century German painting, side by side. UNESCO-protected, hard-won, fully restored.

stops.name: Hackescher Markt
stops.hook: Where the tour ends and Berlin's most photogenic courtyards begin. Coffee, S-Bahn, your next adventure.
